summary: Martin Fowler argues that open source development functions as a research community where developers explore ideas through code rather than academic papers. He observes that the most interesting innovations in enterprise Java have emerged from open source projects. At Thoughtworks, this approach aligns with their philosophy of open intellectual property, where building things serves as a form of publishing. Fowler hopes to eventually give employees dedicated work time for this R&D activity, rather than relegating it to spare time.
key_points: Open source projects serve as a research mechanism where developers explore ideas through programming rather than traditional academic publication
The enterprise Java ecosystem's most significant innovations (Hibernate, JUnit, Spring, etc.) have emerged from open source rather than commercial development
Projects that fade away before reaching version 1.0 are not failures but natural outcomes of an R&D process where exploration matters more than completion rates
Success in R&D should be measured by the quality and impact of successful products, not by the proportion of ideas that become products
money_quotes: That's a notion that sounds strange if you believe that design and programming are separated, but makes a lot of sense if you accept that they are tied together.
We don't have the people who are interested in writing lots of papers for academic conferences - we tend to have people who want to build things. So the open source world is their way of publishing.
A successful R&D isn't measured by what proportion of their ideas turn into products, but rather by how many great products they generate and how great they are.
Someone who starts three projects and turns them all into mediocre products isn't as good as someone who starts a dozen projects and turns only one of them into a killer app.
key_insight: Open source development is fundamentally a research activity where programming serves as the medium for exploring and publishing ideas, and abandoned projects are natural byproducts of healthy R&D rather than failures.
